counties.cherokee-county: When a North Georgia storm line rakes across Cherokee County and leaves trees on roofs, across driveways, and down on power lines, Elite Catastrophe is the crew that answers. We are a family-owned company based in Rome, serving Cherokee County and its towns from Canton and Woodstock to Ball Ground and Nelson. Storm response is where we lead. The county carries a heavy tree load, from mature oaks over older neighborhoods to tall leftover pines in the newer subdivisions, all rooted in red clay that softens after a long rain, and when straight-line winds and hail get serious those trees fail into houses. Our emergency crews work structures first: the tree through the bedroom, the limb pinning a car, the trunk blocking the only way out. That line is answered around the clock. The rest of what we do is here year round too: tree removal, tree trimming, stump grinding, storm damage cleanup, and roof tarping when a falling tree opens up the roof.
counties.paulding-county: Paulding County has filled in fast on the western side of metro Atlanta, trading open ground for wooded subdivisions where pines and hardwoods now stand close to houses. When a spring or summer storm saturates the red clay, pushes straight-line winds through, and drops trees on homes, driveways, and power lines, Elite Catastrophe answers. We are a family-owned company based in Rome, serving Dallas, Hiram, and the rest of Paulding County. Storm response leads the way here, and our emergency crews work structures first every time: trees on houses, limbs over doorways, trunks across the only road out. Those calls are answered around the clock. The county's red clay is the quiet culprit, holding water long after the rain stops so a loblolly pine has little to grip when the wind leans on it. Beyond the emergency, the full service list stays available year round: tree removal, tree trimming, stump grinding, storm damage cleanup, and roof tarping when the roof takes a hit.

canton.narrative: The pattern is familiar across North Georgia. A hot afternoon, a wall of storms building to the west, then a violent stretch of wind and hail that leaves Canton homeowners staring at a tree where a tree should not be. As the seat of Cherokee County, sitting on the Etowah River amid wooded hills, Canton mixes a historic downtown with rings of newer subdivisions, and both carry serious tree load. The older streets hold mature oaks that have long spread over the rooftops. The newer neighborhoods were carved out of pine stands, leaving tall loblollies rooted in red clay that turns soft after a long rain. When a storm hits Canton, those are the trees that fail: pines that snap or uproot, big hardwoods that drop limbs or split, and leaners that end up leaning on a house. Elite Catastrophe is a family-owned company based in Rome, serving Canton and the rest of Cherokee County. Storm response is where we lead, and when severe weather crosses the county our emergency crews work structures first, meaning the trees that have landed on homes, cars, and roads come before anything cosmetic. That line is answered around the clock, because a tree through the roof at two in the morning cannot wait for business hours. When the roof itself is opened up, we tarp it to keep the weather out until repairs can follow. The storm is the headline, but the everyday work is available year round: tree removal, tree trimming, stump grinding, and cleanup of whatever a storm leaves behind. Wondering about a leaning pine on a calm afternoon in Canton? That is a call worth making before the sky turns green.
canton.treeAngle: county seat canopy over Etowah River subdivisions

woodstock.narrative: Woodstock grew from a small railroad town into one of the largest suburbs in Cherokee County, and the older sections kept their trees the whole way. Heavy hardwood canopy shades the established neighborhoods, while the fast-growing subdivisions around them hold the tall pines left behind when construction cleared the trees that once sheltered them. That is a lot of wood over a lot of rooftops, and North Georgia storms know how to find it. When straight-line winds and hail move through, the failures come in a hurry: a loblolly uprooted out of saturated clay and laid across a driveway, an oak limb punched through a garage, a whole tree leaned onto the power line. That is when Elite Catastrophe gets the call. We are a family-owned company based in Rome, serving Woodstock and the surrounding Cherokee County. Storm response leads what we do here, handled structures first: the trees that have hit homes and vehicles or blocked roads come first, in the order that gets people safe and moving again. Those emergency calls are answered around the clock and year round, through the night and through the weekend, because that is when the wind does its damage. If a falling tree opens the roof, we tarp it so the next band of rain stays outside. Once the emergency passes, the same crew handles the everyday list any time you need it: tree removal, tree trimming, stump grinding, and hauling off what the storm cracked or killed. The storm is what most people call about in Woodstock, but a questionable tree over your roof on a calm week is reason enough too.
woodstock.treeAngle: heavy leftover canopy over fast-growing wooded subdivisions

holly-springs.narrative: Follow I-575 north through Cherokee County and Holly Springs is the growing town on either side of it, filling in with wooded neighborhoods where pines and hardwoods stand tight against new homes. That growth is why storm season matters so much here. Every subdivision carved into the woods leaves tall trees behind, and tall trees in saturated red clay are exactly what North Georgia's straight-line winds go after. The storm math is unforgiving. A loblolly pine that grew up sheltered inside a stand is left tall and exposed once construction takes away its neighbors, and after a week of rain its roots lose their grip in soft clay. One hard gust and it is across the driveway, on the roof, or hung up on the service line. Big oaks fail differently, shedding heavy limbs or splitting at weak unions, but the result on the ground looks the same. Elite Catastrophe answers those calls as a family-owned company based in Rome, serving Holly Springs and the rest of Cherokee County. Storm response is our lead work, run structures first: the trees down on houses, cars, and roads clear before anything else, with emergency service around the clock. When a limb or trunk punches through the roof, we tarp it to hold back the weather until repairs happen. The storm is the headline along the I-575 corridor, but the full list stays available year round: tree removal, tree trimming, stump grinding, and storm damage cleanup once the pressure is off. A tree you have been eyeing since the last blow is worth a look before the next one arrives.
holly-springs.treeAngle: I-575 corridor pines over growing wooded neighborhoods

ball-ground.narrative: Up in the north end of Cherokee County, where the land starts to climb toward the mountains, Ball Ground keeps the feel of a small historic town wrapped in woods. The trees here are a mix of tall pines and mature hardwoods, many of them on wooded acreage and ridge-edge lots where the wind has a clear run at them. That exposure changes how storms play out. When a North Georgia system comes through with straight-line winds and hail, a tree on a slope or an open ridge shoulder catches far more force than one tucked into town, and saturated red clay does the rest, letting a loblolly's roots slip until the whole tree goes over. What lands is the emergency: a pine dropped across the driveway, a hardwood limb through the roof, a trunk leaned onto the power line with the lights out behind it. That is the work Elite Catastrophe answers in Ball Ground. We are a family-owned company based in Rome, serving Ball Ground and the north end of Cherokee County, and storm response is where we lead. Our crews run structures first, clearing trees off homes, vehicles, and roads ahead of anything else, with emergency calls answered around the clock and year round. If the roof is opened up in the process, we tarp it to keep the rain out. Once the hazards are down, the same crew handles the ordinary work whenever it suits you: tree removal, tree trimming, stump grinding, and cleanup of storm-damaged wood. In a place as wooded and wind-exposed as Ball Ground, the storm call earns its keep, but a leaning tree on a quiet day is just as welcome.
ball-ground.treeAngle: north Cherokee hardwoods and pines near the mountains

waleska.narrative: Waleska is a small, rural town in Cherokee County, best known as the home of Reinhardt University and wrapped in the kind of wooded, open acreage that gives big trees plenty of room to grow. That same room means a lot of tall timber standing close to homes, barns, and drives, and when a storm hits Waleska, all that wood becomes the problem. The failures follow the regional script. Loblolly pines, shallow-rooted in red clay that softens after days of rain, snap off or heave out of the ground when straight-line winds hit. Old oaks and other hardwoods shed heavy limbs or come apart at the trunk. On rural lots the fallen tree often takes a fence, a shed, or a stretch of driveway with it, and sometimes the power line goes down under the weight. Elite Catastrophe is a family-owned company based in Rome, serving Waleska and the rest of Cherokee County. Storm response leads the way, and structures first is how we work: trees off houses, vehicles, and roads before anything else, with emergency calls answered around the clock and year round because storms here do not keep office hours. When a tree tears open the roof, we tarp it to keep the inside dry until repairs can be made. After the urgent work, the everyday services stay available on your schedule: tree removal, tree trimming, stump grinding, and storm damage cleanup so the property is usable again. Waleska's headline is storm response, but the same crew is glad to look at a tree on an ordinary afternoon, long before the radar lights up.
waleska.treeAngle: rural wooded acreage around Reinhardt University

nelson.narrative: Nelson sits right on the Cherokee and Pickens county line, a small town in old marble country where the surroundings are rural and heavily wooded. It is a quiet place most of the year. Then North Georgia's storm season arrives, and the quiet gets interrupted by the sound of trees coming down. The setup around Nelson is all tall timber and open ground. Loblolly pines stand in red clay that turns soft after a long soaking rain, and when straight-line winds and hail push through, those pines snap or lift out at the roots. Mature hardwoods fail too, dropping big limbs or splitting where the trunk forks. On these rural properties a falling tree tends to find something: a roofline, a vehicle, a driveway, or the power line running out to the house. When that happens, Elite Catastrophe answers. We are a family-owned company based in Rome, serving Nelson and the north end of Cherokee County. Storm response is our lead work, run structures first: the trees down on homes, cars, and roads come off ahead of anything else, and emergency calls are answered around the clock and year round. If the roof is punctured, we tarp it to hold back the next round of weather. Once the immediate danger is cleared, the same crew takes care of the rest whenever it suits you: tree removal, tree trimming, stump grinding, and cleanup of the wood a storm leaves behind. In a rural, wooded town like Nelson the storm is the reason most people call, but a declining tree near the house is worth handling on a calm day, well before it picks its own moment.
nelson.treeAngle: rural wooded lots in old marble country

dallas.narrative: Ask anyone in Dallas who has been through a rough Paulding County storm season and they can describe the aftermath: driveways blocked, limbs down across the yard, and at least one tree leaning somewhere new. As the Paulding County seat, Dallas has grown quickly, its wooded subdivisions spreading across the red clay hills that define this part of Georgia. All that new construction left tall trees standing close to new houses, and that is a setup storms know how to exploit. The clay is the quiet villain. It holds water long after the rain stops, and a loblolly pine rooted in saturated ground has very little to hang onto when straight-line winds arrive. Over it goes, across a driveway or onto a roof. The big hardwoods fail on their own terms, shedding heavy limbs without warning or splitting apart in a strong gust. Elite Catastrophe is a family-owned company based in Rome, serving Dallas and the rest of Paulding County. Storm response is where we lead: when severe weather crosses the county, our crews work structures first, clearing the trees that have landed on homes, vehicles, and roads before anything else, with emergency calls answered around the clock. When a tree opens up the roof, we tarp it to keep the weather out until repairs follow. The storm is the headline, but the everyday work stays available year round: tree removal, tree trimming, stump grinding, and storm damage cleanup so the yard is whole again. In a fast-growing county seat like Dallas, plenty of calls come with a tree already down, but a leaning pine on a calm afternoon is exactly the one we would rather see first.
dallas.treeAngle: Paulding seat subdivisions rooted in red clay

hiram.narrative: Hiram grew up along US-278 as a retail hub for Paulding County, and the neighborhoods spreading out from that busy corridor are full of the pines and hardwoods that come standard in North Georgia. Those trees are pleasant to live under right up until a storm turns them into a hazard, and around Hiram the storms come every season. The physics are always the same. A stretch of heavy rain saturates the red clay, straight-line winds and hail arrive on a hot afternoon, and the tall loblolly pines left standing when the subdivisions went in start to give. Some snap partway up. Others lift out at the roots and fall whole, onto a roof, across a driveway, or down onto the power line. The oaks and other hardwoods add their own damage, dropping limbs heavy enough to crush whatever is beneath them. Elite Catastrophe answers Hiram as a family-owned company based in Rome, serving Hiram and the rest of Paulding County. Storm response leads what we do, worked structures first: the trees that have hit houses, vehicles, and roads get cleared first, with emergency calls answered around the clock and year round whenever the storms decide to move. When a limb drives through the roof, we tarp the opening to keep the rain outside until repairs can be made. Once the danger is off the house and out of the road, the follow-up runs on your timeline: tree removal, tree trimming, stump grinding, and storm damage cleanup. Along the US-278 corridor, many calls come with a tree already down, but the everyday tree work is here year round, and a questionable tree is always worth a look before the next storm.
hiram.treeAngle: US-278 hub pines and hardwoods over homes
